brands.description: Airbnb is a website for people to list, find, and rent lodging. It has over 1,500,000 listings in 34,000 cities and 190 countries. Founded in August 2008 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, the company is privately owned and operated by Airbnb, Inc.
brands.name: Airbnb

brands.description: Esselunga S.p.A. is an Italian retail store chain. Founded in 1957 by brothers Bernardo, Guido and Claudio of the Caprotti family with Nelson Rockefeller, the company is now entirely owned by the Caprotti family through Supermarkets Italiani S.p.A.
brands.name: Esselunga

brands.description: Enel is an Italian multinational manufacturer and distributor of electricity and gas. Enel, which originally stood for National Entity for Electricity (Ente nazionale per l'energia elettrica), was first established as a public body at the end of 1962, and then transformed into a limited company in 1992. In 1999, following the liberalization of the electricity market in Italy, Enel was privatized.
brands.name: Enel

brands.description: Wells Fargo & Company is an American multinational banking and financial services holding company headquartered in San Francisco, California, with "hubquarters" throughout the country. It is the third largest bank in the U.S. by assets and the largest bank by market capitalization. Wells Fargo surpassed Citigroup Inc. to become the third-largest U.S. bank by assets at the end of 2015.
brands.name: Wells Fargo

brands.description: Informa plc is a multinational publishing and events company with its head office and registered office in London. It has offices in 43 countries and around 6,500 employees. It owns numerous brands including CRC Press, Datamonitor, Institute for International Research, Lloyd's List (London Press Lloyd), Routledge, and Taylor & Francis.
brands.name: Informa

namedLocations.description: Milan (/mɪˈlæn/, also US: /mɪˈlɑːn/, Milanese: [miˈlãː]; Italian: Milano [miˈlaːno] ) is a city in northern Italy, capital of Lombardy, and the second-most populous city in Italy after Rome, with the city proper having a population of 1,395,274 while its metropolitan city has a population of 3,245,308. Its continuously built-up urban area (that stretches beyond the boundaries of the Metropolitan City of Milan) has a population estimated to be about 5,270,000 over 1,891 square kilometres (730 square miles). The wider Milan metropolitan area, known as Greater Milan, is a polycentric metropolitan region that extends over central Lombardy and eastern Piedmont and which counts an estimated total population of 7.5 million, making it by far the largest metropolitan area in Italy and the 54th largest in the world. Milan served as capital of the Western Roman Empire from 286 to 402 and the Duchy of Milan during the medieval period and early modern age.
namedLocations.name: Milan

namedLocations.description: Lausanne (/loʊˈzæn/, French: [lozan], German: [loˈzan]; Arpitan: Losena, [lɔˈzəna] ; Italian: Losanna; Romansh: Losanna) is the capital city and biggest town of the canton of Vaud in Romandy, Switzerland. A municipality, it is situated on the shores of Lake Geneva (French: Le/Lac Léman). It faces the French town of Évian-les-Bains, with the Jura Mountains to its north-west. Lausanne is located 62 kilometres (38.5 miles) northeast of Geneva.
namedLocations.name: Lausanne

namedLocations.description: Gangneung ([kaŋ.nɯŋ]) is a city in the province of Gangwon-do, on the east coast of South Korea. It has a population of 213,658 (as of 2017). Gangneung is the economic centre of the Yeongdong region of Gangwon-do. Gangneung has many tourist attractions, such as Jeongdongjin, a very popular area for watching the sun rise, and Gyeongpo Beach. There is an ROK airbase south of downtown Gangneung that formerly doubled as a civil airport.
namedLocations.name: Gangneung

namedLocations.description: Esselunga S.p.A. is an Italian retail store chain. Founded in 1957 by brothers Bernardo, Guido and Claudio of the Caprotti family with Nelson Rockefeller, the company is now entirely owned by the Caprotti family through Supermarkets Italiani S.p.A.
namedLocations.name: Esselunga

namedLocations.description: A podcast is a digital medium consisting of an episodic series of audio, digital radio, PDF, or ePub files subscribed to and downloaded through web syndication or streamed online to a computer or mobile device. The word is a neologism and portmanteau derived from "broadcast" and "pod" from the success of the iPod, as audio podcasts are often listened to on portable media players. Merriam Webster defines Podcast: a program made available in digital format for automatic download over the Internet. A list of all the audio or video files associated with a given series is maintained centrally on the distributor's server as a web feed, and the listener or viewer employs special client application software, known as a podcatcher, that can access this web feed, check it for updates, and download any new files in the series. This process can be automated so that new files are downloaded automatically, which may seem to the user as if the content is being broadcast or "pushed" to them. Files are stored locally on the user's computer or other device ready for offline use, giving simple and convenient access to the content.
namedLocations.name: Podcast

namedPeople.description: William "Bill" L. Daly III (born May 1, 1964) is an American attorney and the current deputy commissioner and chief legal officer of the National Hockey League (NHL) under commissioner, Gary Bettman. He is also a Hockey Hall of Fame board member and former NHL vice president and chief legal officer. Before joining the NHL front office he worked for the New York law firm of Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom, LLP. He received his bachelor's degree from Dartmouth College and his law degree from New York University School of Law.
namedPeople.name: Bill Daly

namedPeople.description: Braeden Tkachuk (born September 16, 1999) is an American professional ice hockey winger for the Ottawa Senators of the National Hockey League (NHL). Tkachuk was chosen by the Senators as the fourth overall pick in the 2018 NHL Entry Draft. Prior to turning professional, Tkachuk played one season for the Boston University Terriers, earning All-Hockey East Rookie Team honors.
namedPeople.name: Brady Tkachuk

namedPeople.description: Hockey left winger who was drafted 6th overall in the 2016 NHL Entry Draft. In the months immediately preceding the draft, he was ranked as the #2 North American skater available for drafting.
namedPeople.name: Matthew Tkachuk

namedPeople.description: Dallas Cowboys running back who retired with an NFL-record 18,355 rushing yards. He was selected to eight Pro Bowls, led the NFL in rushing four times, and won three Super Bowl Championships. After retirement, he won the third season of Dancing with the Stars with Cheryl Burke.
namedPeople.name: Emmitt Smith

transcript.text: In January of 2017, construction finished on the Ginyung Hockey Center just over a year before the Pyeongchang Olympics.

On January 19th, 2026, just 17 days before the 1st hockey game at the 2026 Milan Cortina Olympics, the guy in charge of the ice at the Santa Julia Ice Hockey Arena in Milan described the arena as quote.
It's just, there's just mud everywhere.
On January 31st, it was confirmed that the venue would not, in fact, be done on time.

This thing was so unbelievably delayed and unbuilt ahead of the games that there was concern it wouldn't be usable at all, or that the NHL might pull their players from the Olympics over safety concerns.
Still, by the time this video comes out, there will probably have already been multiple hockey games played, I hope without incidents in Santa Julia Arena.
So what went wrong?
And then, right?
Our story starts as many calamities due in 2019, when Milan and Cortina dem Pazo were selected as the host cities for the 2026 Winter Olympics.
To their credit, they did have a plan to get a hockey arena built.
Events company CTS Eventem would develop an arena in Santa Julia, Milan that the Olympics could lease as an ice hockey arena before it became a permanent concert venue.
In fact, it was the only permanent venue plan for these games, which you think would mean there'd be time to get it done.
But in 2020, the craziest thing happened.
I don't remember what it was, you probably don't either, but it was definitely a problem in Milan.
The pandemic, which to be fair delayed a lot of things, delayed plans to start building the venue and also drove up construction costs.
Costs that were driven even higher by Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and the war that ensued.
The initial plan was to build a 210 ish $1,000,000 arena starting in the fall of 2022 and finishing by the fall of 2025.
What they actually did was sort of build a nearly $300 million arena starting in spring of 2023 and finishing.
We all hope eventually for the Olympics the arena needed two things.
One, to be a functional arena with places for spectators, press players, etcetera to sit, eat, go to the bathroom and generally be encased within walls and roof.
And 2A game ready ice hockey rink, which is no easy task.
You need to build a big concrete slab with pipes inside to circulate liquid cold enough to freeze water without itself freezing, which can maintain a consistent temperature across its entire surface.
Then you got to mist down and freeze dozens of millimeter thick layers of ice to make sure you end up with an appropriately hard air bubble free hockey, safe sheet of ice.

Building a decent rink is obviously important on its own, but even more so here because the National Hockey League, whose season the Winter Olympics lands smack in the middle of, is lending their players out to the Olympics and threatened that they pulled him out if the Santa Julia ice wasn't up to snuff.
NHL have only been at 5 Olympics, with the most recent before 2026 being the 2014 Sochi Games.
Since then, the NHL, the NHL players union, the international governing body of hockey, and the IOC couldn't come to an agreement that allowed NHL players to play.
That's because letting them do so is, in many ways, not great for the league.
The Olympics fall right in the middle of the regular NHL season and right after the end of the American football season, IE a great time to capture some wayward football fan eyeballs.
In effectively leasing players to the Olympics, the NHL creates a huge gap in their schedule and takes on the risk that their best of the best players might get injured while they're there.
Players that are being paid millions upon millions of dollars to play hockey in the NHL.
So there's some hesitance to send their most valuable assets to Italy for three weeks.
However, those valuable assets really vocally want to play for their national teams.
Not to mention the Olympics fit nicely into the NHL stated desire to grow the game internationally.

So with all that in mind, they reached an agreement for the Milan Olympics only to find out as of a few months before the 1st puck drop that the rank would be 3 feet shorter than they'd sign up for in the agreement.
And also their owners weren't getting the hospitality suite on site they were promised, and also the arena wasn't done yet.
Whoops.
The first Test matches at the Santa Julia Arena were supposed to be played in December of 2025, which is pretty late, by the way.
The Bolshoi Ice Dome, built for 2014, hosted games as early as April 2013 and held an entire tournament in the December preceding the Olympics.

But in December of 2025, not only were the Santa Julia Arena test games cancelled, presumably due to the place not being built yet, the NHL Board of Governors threatened to pull NHL players out of the Olympics if the ice wasn't safe to play on.
Not that the players seem to care, but it's not up to them.
And to be clear here, the NHL didn't want to pull players.
In fact, when word got out about how not built to the arena was, the NHL sent some of their top ice technicians over to Italy to pull this thing together.
The first skaters touched Santa Julia Arena ice on January 9th, when the still under construction arena hosted a handful of Italian Cup games to simulate Olympic Level 3 games a day ice use.
At that point, just three of the Plant 14 ringside dressing rooms were ready.
The Zamboni ramp was just two days old, and the first Test game was delayed by 5:00-ish minutes to repair a hole in the ice, reportedly part of a wider soft spot in front of one of the Nets.
Over the course of three days, the ice held up and there were no further delays for hole patching.
And while players reported the ice was a bit uneven, with some spots harder or softer than others, everyone seemed to have walked away confident that the ice itself would be ready for Olympic play.

All that was left was for Dom Moffett, the ice maker for both the Colorado Avalanche and the last five Olympic ice hockey rinks, to shave down the top layer, repaint the ice white and add the lines in the Olympic logo, then refreeze the new top layers.
All of which he would usually do about two months beforehand.
This time he got 3 weeks.
The test games made people feel better about the ice and satisfy the NHL, but it did not necessarily inspire confidence in the rest of the arena.
I eat the parts to be enjoyed by people without ice skates on.
The top 2 tiers of seating were blocked off by plastic sheeting, the digital scoreboard wasn't up and the Jumbotron was teeny tiny and so high their players couldn't see the clock.
Also, there was no paint on the floor and dust all around, wire sticking out of the walls, and worst of all, no concession stand.
Where the heck am I going to get my traditional second period risotto Ala Milanese or my hot dog?

In an effort to have things more Olympic ready by the first game on February 5th, they put 1600 workers a day alternating round the clock shifts, which per the ice guy is hard to convince local workers to do even with more pay.
Versus here in America where you can convince people to pull 100 hour work week on little more than the promise that it will build character and net them some kind of plaque.
Just ask my writers or their plaques.
In the end, tons of concessions were made to get the building around the rink quasi functional.
Or rather, very few concessions are being made because concessions are largely being outsourced to food trucks parked outside.
They also cut down their seating capacity from the planned 14,000 and 12 to just under 12,000.
At the time of writing, there weren't yet chairs in the press area, the toilets hadn't been unwrapped, and it was still garbage and construction detritus everywhere.
And there may be a metaphorical or literal paint drying during the first Olympic game in the arena.
Even beyond here on February 2nd when this is being written.
I don't know.
What I do know is that the Austrian women's team is going to shock the world and take home the gold.
Note to editor.
Cut that line if it doesn't happen.
I can't afford to look dumb online.
And hey, if you don't want to look dumb online, maybe you should avoid having your identity stolen with the help of this video sponsor, Nord VPN.
We all know cybersecurity is really important, and Nord makes it easy to keep yourself and your data safe online.
It's especially good if, like me, you travel a lot.
I consider myself decently Internet savvy, but hackers have gotten even savvier.

For example, if I'm at a free Wi-Fi access point at a coffee shop in France, that Wi-Fi could actually be a person posing as an impostor, and then they have access to all my data while I'm using that service.

That's why I use Nord VPN, which gives me Online Protection with a single click by encrypting all of my information, regardless if the connection is secure or not, and with an added layer of security through Nord VPN Threat Protection Pro, I'm also safe from scams and malware.
My job as a creator relies on the Internet, and it relies on it being safe.
So knowing that I have Nord VPN as a barrier to online threats, censorship and surveillance is a huge relief for me both at home and on the go.
The good news is that you can try nord.vpnwithnorisk@nordvpn.com/half as Interesting or click the link in the description to get four months free with your two year plan.
And Nord has a 30 day money back guarantee so you can try it for yourself.
But like I said, it gives me a sense of peace when travelling to know my information to secure.
And you can get that too by visiting nordvpn.com/half as Interesting.

Construction itself may not be completed by the time the women's tournament starts, but for Don Moffatt, the chief ice maker for ice
hockey at the 2026 Winter Olympics, all he can do is focus on what's in his control.
Posted 10:30 AM, Jan 19, 2026 and last updated 11:21 AM, Jan 19, 2026
MILAN, Italy - For Don Moffatt, all he can worry about is what's in his control. As the
2026 Winter Olympics Chief Ice Maker-also sometimes referred to as the "Ice
Master"-Moffatt has only concerned himself with making the ice hockey playing
"This is my fifth Olympics. It's been by far the hardest, but I've done a lot of special
Furthermore, the Olympic venue drew additional outside criticism when it was
revealed it's three feet shorter than a typical NHL rink, but the NHL Players
Association (NHLPA) has stated players don't expect it to have a pronounced impact
After last weekend's test event, the NHL and NHLPA issued a joint statement in
support of the ability to compete on schedule inside the arena.
"While challenges are inherent with new ice and a still-under-construction venue, we
expect that the work necessary to address all remaining issues will continue around
the clock," they said in a statement sent to the AP. "The NHL and NHLPA will
continue to monitor the situation, standing ready to consult and advise on the work
being done to ensure that the local organizing committee, the IOC [International
Olympic Committee], and the IIHF [International Ice Hockey Federation] deliver a
tournament and playing conditions befitting the world's best players."
Moffatt said he knows the ice is the center point and called the process a "huge
challenge" with the construction crews. Following the test event last weekend, Moffatt
said he ceded the venue to the workers in the hopes they can finish as much as they
"I basically gave everybody free reign this week with the threat of they better get their
work done this week over top of the ice surface, because starting on Monday, I'll be
preparing the sheet for the Olympics," he said. "I was in yesterday and it's just an
absolute free-for-all. It's just, there's just mud everywhere. It's been raining here, and
Moffatt also attributed a difference in culture between Canadian and American
workers compared to their European and Italian counterparts.
Whereas construction crews in North America might work fast-paced, around the
clock to finish a project in a crunch like this, he said the Italian workers are more

By Wes Crosby / NHL.com Independent Correspondent
PITTSBURGH -- Brady Tkachuk wants to turn a dream into reality.
"It's something I've been wanting my whole life," Dallas Stars and Team USA goalie Jake
Oettinger said. "To finally get that call, it is one of the best things to ever happen."
"We're going there, and all 12 nations want that gold medal," Tkachuk told NHL.com on Monday. "So that's
something that, it's been a childhood dream of, not just mine, but by every Olympian, that they want to come
out with gold. Really believe in our team, really believe in the build, the makeup."
"It gives me shivers," Tampa Bay Lightning forward Brandon Hagel, who will be representing
Canada, said regarding his selection to the Olympic roster. "Being able to play for the flag is a
dream come true. I never would have thought about it in a million years, and now, obviously,

MILAN - The coldest place to stand in the bone-chilling Santagiulia Arena
this weekend was right beside me.
Organizers didn't exactly warm up to a visitor from Canada who made the
trip over to lay on eyes on the still-under-construction primary hockey
venue for the Milan Cortina Olympic tournament, particularly after calling
attention to a five-plus minute delay in Friday's opening test game to repair
They objected to the fact that I referred to it as a "giant hole" in a post on X.
A large soft spot in front of one of the nets was evident to the naked eve
before the puck even dropped on an Italian Cup game between two second
division men's teams. That informed my characterization when the game
was delayed and an official skated onto the surface carrying a green
watering can to pour water on that exact area.

Many areas of the arena, including hospitality boxes as well as food and drink

brands.name: Airbnb
brands.description: Airbnb is a website for people to list, find, and rent lodging. It has over 1,500,000 listings in 34,000 cities and 190 countries. Founded in August 2008 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, the company is privately owned and operated by Airbnb, Inc.

brands.name: Informa
brands.description: Informa plc is a multinational publishing and events company with its head office and registered office in London. It has offices in 43 countries and around 6,500 employees. It owns numerous brands including CRC Press, Datamonitor, Institute for International Research, Lloyd's List (London Press Lloyd), Routledge, and Taylor & Francis.

brands.name: Esselunga
brands.description: Esselunga S.p.A. is an Italian retail store chain. Founded in 1957 by brothers Bernardo, Guido and Claudio of the Caprotti family with Nelson Rockefeller, the company is now entirely owned by the Caprotti family through Supermarkets Italiani S.p.A.

brands.name: Wells Fargo
brands.description: Wells Fargo & Company is an American multinational banking and financial services holding company headquartered in San Francisco, California, with "hubquarters" throughout the country. It is the third largest bank in the U.S. by assets and the largest bank by market capitalization. Wells Fargo surpassed Citigroup Inc. to become the third-largest U.S. bank by assets at the end of 2015.

brands.name: Enel
brands.description: Enel is an Italian multinational manufacturer and distributor of electricity and gas. Enel, which originally stood for National Entity for Electricity (Ente nazionale per l'energia elettrica), was first established as a public body at the end of 1962, and then transformed into a limited company in 1992. In 1999, following the liberalization of the electricity market in Italy, Enel was privatized.

namedLocations.name: Gangneung
namedLocations.description: Gangneung ([kaŋ.nɯŋ]) is a city in the province of Gangwon-do, on the east coast of South Korea. It has a population of 213,658 (as of 2017). Gangneung is the economic centre of the Yeongdong region of Gangwon-do. Gangneung has many tourist attractions, such as Jeongdongjin, a very popular area for watching the sun rise, and Gyeongpo Beach. There is an ROK airbase south of downtown Gangneung that formerly doubled as a civil airport.

namedLocations.name: Milan
namedLocations.description: Milan (/mɪˈlæn/, also US: /mɪˈlɑːn/, Milanese: [miˈlãː]; Italian: Milano [miˈlaːno] ) is a city in northern Italy, capital of Lombardy, and the second-most populous city in Italy after Rome, with the city proper having a population of 1,395,274 while its metropolitan city has a population of 3,245,308. Its continuously built-up urban area (that stretches beyond the boundaries of the Metropolitan City of Milan) has a population estimated to be about 5,270,000 over 1,891 square kilometres (730 square miles). The wider Milan metropolitan area, known as Greater Milan, is a polycentric metropolitan region that extends over central Lombardy and eastern Piedmont and which counts an estimated total population of 7.5 million, making it by far the largest metropolitan area in Italy and the 54th largest in the world. Milan served as capital of the Western Roman Empire from 286 to 402 and the Duchy of Milan during the medieval period and early modern age.

namedLocations.name: Lausanne
namedLocations.description: Lausanne (/loʊˈzæn/, French: [lozan], German: [loˈzan]; Arpitan: Losena, [lɔˈzəna] ; Italian: Losanna; Romansh: Losanna) is the capital city and biggest town of the canton of Vaud in Romandy, Switzerland. A municipality, it is situated on the shores of Lake Geneva (French: Le/Lac Léman). It faces the French town of Évian-les-Bains, with the Jura Mountains to its north-west. Lausanne is located 62 kilometres (38.5 miles) northeast of Geneva.

namedLocations.name: Podcast
namedLocations.description: A podcast is a digital medium consisting of an episodic series of audio, digital radio, PDF, or ePub files subscribed to and downloaded through web syndication or streamed online to a computer or mobile device. The word is a neologism and portmanteau derived from "broadcast" and "pod" from the success of the iPod, as audio podcasts are often listened to on portable media players. Merriam Webster defines Podcast: a program made available in digital format for automatic download over the Internet. A list of all the audio or video files associated with a given series is maintained centrally on the distributor's server as a web feed, and the listener or viewer employs special client application software, known as a podcatcher, that can access this web feed, check it for updates, and download any new files in the series. This process can be automated so that new files are downloaded automatically, which may seem to the user as if the content is being broadcast or "pushed" to them. Files are stored locally on the user's computer or other device ready for offline use, giving simple and convenient access to the content.

namedLocations.name: Esselunga
namedLocations.description: Esselunga S.p.A. is an Italian retail store chain. Founded in 1957 by brothers Bernardo, Guido and Claudio of the Caprotti family with Nelson Rockefeller, the company is now entirely owned by the Caprotti family through Supermarkets Italiani S.p.A.

namedPeople.name: Emmitt Smith
namedPeople.description: Dallas Cowboys running back who retired with an NFL-record 18,355 rushing yards. He was selected to eight Pro Bowls, led the NFL in rushing four times, and won three Super Bowl Championships. After retirement, he won the third season of Dancing with the Stars with Cheryl Burke.

namedPeople.name: Bill Daly
namedPeople.description: William "Bill" L. Daly III (born May 1, 1964) is an American attorney and the current deputy commissioner and chief legal officer of the National Hockey League (NHL) under commissioner, Gary Bettman. He is also a Hockey Hall of Fame board member and former NHL vice president and chief legal officer. Before joining the NHL front office he worked for the New York law firm of Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom, LLP. He received his bachelor's degree from Dartmouth College and his law degree from New York University School of Law.

namedPeople.name: Brady Tkachuk
namedPeople.description: Braeden Tkachuk (born September 16, 1999) is an American professional ice hockey winger for the Ottawa Senators of the National Hockey League (NHL). Tkachuk was chosen by the Senators as the fourth overall pick in the 2018 NHL Entry Draft. Prior to turning professional, Tkachuk played one season for the Boston University Terriers, earning All-Hockey East Rookie Team honors.

namedPeople.name: Matthew Tkachuk
namedPeople.description: Hockey left winger who was drafted 6th overall in the 2016 NHL Entry Draft. In the months immediately preceding the draft, he was ranked as the #2 North American skater available for drafting.
